description: This figure presents a schematic diagram of the air induction melting (AIM) process with protective flux cover (AIMFC) developed for producing Fe-Al based lightweight steels. Subfigure (a) illustrates the melting configuration: an alumina-lined crucible (MgO/Al₂O₃ lining, 150 mm diameter, 300 mm total height) equipped with induction coils contains molten metal (240 mm depth) covered by a 2-4 mm thick molten slag layer (Al₂O₃-CaO-MgO-CaF₂ system) that acts as a barrier between the aluminum-containing melt and atmospheric water vapor to prevent hydrogen pickup and gas porosity. Subfigure (b) depicts the pouring stage where the crucible is tilted to transfer molten metal through a tundish into split cast iron molds (80 mm diameter) to form solidified ingots. The caption and text indicate this cost-effective technique involves melting iron first, adding preheated flux, then plunging aluminum pieces into the bath held at 1900 K for 2 minutes, achieving sound, porosity-free Fe-7wt%Al and Fe-9wt%Al alloy ingots with 95-98% aluminum recovery as an alternative to vacuum induction melting (VIM).

This figure presents a thermodynamic chart plotting the standard free energy of formation (ΔF°) as a function of temperature for magnesium oxide (MgO) and aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃). The graph displays two linear curves: open circles represent MgO while filled circles represent Al₂O₃, with temperature ranging from 0 to 2400°C on the x-axis and ΔF° values from -300 to -100 K.Cals on the y-axis. Both oxides show increasing ΔF° (becoming less negative) with rising temperature, but with different slopes causing them to intersect at approximately 1600°C. Below this temperature, MgO exhibits more negative formation energy, indicating greater thermodynamic stability, whereas Al₂O₃ becomes more stable above the intersection point. The caption and citation context indicate this data is critical for crucible lining selection in melting iron aluminides. The curves demonstrate that aluminum in the melt can reduce MgO at temperatures around 1873 K (near the melting point of iron aluminides), leading to magnesium contamination and crucible erosion, thereby supporting the rationale for avoiding MgO crucibles in high-aluminum alloys.

Figure 3 presents macroscopic photographs of cold-rolled Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy products fabricated via the air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC) process. Subfigure (a) displays three flat plates and sheets of varying dimensions with metallic gray surfaces, demonstrating the intermediate thickness stages of thermomechanical processing. Subfigure (b) shows four cylindrical foil specimens of progressively smaller sizes, with a 2 cm scale bar indicating macroscopic dimensions ranging from approximately 4 cm down to 1.5 cm in diameter. These images visually confirm that Fe-7Al alloy ingots produced by the cost-effective AIMFC route can be successfully processed through hot forging, hot rolling, and subsequent cold rolling into thin sheets and flexible foils. The photographs support the key scientific conclusion that AIMFC-processed material exhibits excellent cold workability and room-temperature ductility comparable to vacuum induction melted (VIM) material, validating the industrial viability of this cost-effective melting technique for lightweight steel production.

This figure presents five optical micrographs (a-e) illustrating the microstructural characteristics of hot-rolled Fe-Al lightweight steel alloys produced via different melting routes. The images compare air induction melting (AIM), air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC), and vacuum induction melting (VIM) for alloys containing 7 wt.% and 9 wt.% aluminum. Subfigure (a) shows the coarse-grained microstructure of AIM-processed Fe-7Al, while (b) displays the AIMFC Fe-7Al alloy with similar equiaxed grain morphology. Subfigure (c) reveals the AIMFC Fe-9Al alloy exhibiting coarse grains with visible microcracks attributed to residual hydrogen embrittlement and poor hot workability. Subfigure (d) presents the VIM Fe-7Al alloy with comparable coarse grain structure, and (e) shows the VIM Fe-9Al alloy with coarse equiaxed grains but free of microcracks, indicating excellent hot workability. All alloys display annealed microstructures with grain sizes ranging from approximately 186 μm to 228 μm. The key finding demonstrates that AIMFC produces grain sizes comparable to VIM, validating the flux-cover technique as a cost-effective alternative for Fe-Al alloys, though high aluminum content (9 wt.%) requires stringent hydrogen control to prevent thermomechanical processing defects.

This figure presents optical micrographs showing the microstructural evolution of hot-rolled air induction melted (AIM) Fe-7 wt.% Al plates with varying carbon contents. Subfigure (a) displays the single-phase α-Fe(Al) solid solution structure with 0.012 wt.% C, characterized by coarse equiaxed grains and absence of precipitates. Subfigures (b) through (e) illustrate the progressive development of precipitates with increasing carbon content: (b) 0.15 wt.% C showing initial precipitation at grain boundaries, (c) 0.35 wt.% C with increased precipitate density both at boundaries and within grains, (d) 0.5 wt.% C exhibiting a banded/lamellar structure with aligned precipitates, and (e) 1.0 wt.% C revealing a high volume fraction of Fe3AlC0.5 perovskite precipitates distributed throughout the matrix. The scale bar in (e) indicates 250 μm. These micrographs demonstrate that carbon addition significantly alters the microstructure, transitioning from a single-phase disordered solid solution at low carbon levels to a two-phase structure with substantial Fe3AlC0.5 precipitation at higher carbon concentrations, thereby supporting the strengthening mechanism in Fe-Al-C lightweight steels.

The figure presents two X-ray diffraction (XRD) traces using Cu-Kα radiation comparing Fe-7 wt.% Al alloys with different carbon contents to identify phase formation. Subfigure (a) displays the diffraction pattern of Fe-7 wt.% Al-0.012 wt.% C, showing peaks indexed exclusively to the Fe3Al phase including (111), (220), (400), (422), and (440) reflections, indicating a single-phase disordered solid solution. Subfigure (b) shows the pattern for Fe-7 wt.% Al-0.15 wt.% C, where additional peaks corresponding to the Fe3AlC0.5 perovskite carbide phase appear at approximately 44-45° (200) and 82-83° (311), coexisting with the Fe3Al matrix peaks. The caption notes that similar Fe3AlC0.5 peaks were observed in alloys with carbon contents of 0.35–1 wt.%. These patterns demonstrate that carbon additions above ~0.15 wt.% induce precipitation of Fe3AlC0.5 in the Fe-Al matrix, confirming that carbon promotes precipitation hardening and microstructural evolution in these lightweight steels.
